category_title: Preschoolers at risk for developmental delays with mild language disorders
minimal_context: The student and a peer are playing with dramatic play toys and cars on a carpeted floor during a free play session.
dialogue:
TUTOR: It is time for free play. You and your partner can play with the dramatic play toys and cars on the carpet.

STUDENT: (Sits on the carpet, appearing lonely and making no comments to the peer.)

TUTOR: What are you going to play?

STUDENT: (Looks at the peer) Can I have the car?

TUTOR: Keep playing together.

STUDENT: (Does not engage in play with the peer) Can I have the car? Can I have the car? Can I have the car?

options.A: Spend the free play period working on projects, covertly listening to and watching the student and peer without engaging in play. Intervene minimally, only for disputes or extreme raucousness.
options.B: Show the student a brief video clip before free play begins, and discuss how friends respectfully talk to one another, compliment one another, and ask to help each other.
options.C: Empower the peer to exclude the student from the dramatic play area during the session if they continue to exhibit challenging or repetitive behaviors instead of engaging appropriately.
options.D: Utilize regulated settings that allow the student and peer to acquire skills for sustained social engagement, such as developing a play plan, acquiring specific phrases or actions, and reflecting on the interaction.
